target_game.name: Knock'em All
target_game.core_loop: Tap to shoot balls at approaching dummies, using physics to knock them off platforms and progress to the next level.
target_game.audience_proxy: Broad casual audience, all ages, looking for quick, satisfying physics-based action.
target_game.visual_style: Minimalist 3D with bright, flat colors and simple geometric environments.

palette.description: Bright, airy pastel backgrounds contrasting with vibrant primary colors for enemies and weapons.

centroid_hook.visual_action: Goku is depicted charging a blue energy blast against a vibrant, rainbow-colored background with glowing orbs.
centroid_hook.text_overlay: Dragon Ball Z Dokkan Battle PLAY NOW!

top_archetypes.rationale: 1 creatives across 1 unique advertisers, average age 620d (velocity source: sov). Hook representative: "Static character art of Goku Ultra Instinct with a prominent 'PLAY NOW!' call to"

top_archetypes.label: Fail · in-game

centroid_hook.summary: A player attempts a difficult level and immediately fails by crashing a bus.

top_archetypes.rationale: 1 creatives across 1 unique advertisers, average age 259d (velocity source: sov). Hook representative: "A player attempts a difficult level and immediately fails by crashing a bus."

game_fit_scores.rationale: This is a licensed-IP anime hook (Dokkan Battle/Goku) — visually saturated neon anime art clashes hard with Knock'em All's minimalist pastel 3D look, and we obviously can't use Dragon Ball IP without licensing. The static "PLAY NOW" charging-blast concept also doesn't communicate the tap-to-shoot physics ragdoll loop that drives installs for this game, and it skews to a mid-core anime gacha audience rather than the broad casual hypercasual player. Skip — neither the asset nor the concept is portable; if there's a learning to extract, it's only the very generic "bright character + PLAY NOW CTA" pattern, which we already cover with on-brand creatives.

Hard pass — this archetype is licensed Dragon Ball Z anime IP (Goku SSJ3 Dragon Fist), which creates immediate legal/trademark risk and zero authentic ties to Knock'em All's minimalist 3D physics-shooter DNA. The dark anime palette (gold/navy/indigo) and shonen action audience clash with the game's bright pastel, broad-casual, calm/satisfying tone, and a charged-punch dragon attack has no analog to tap-to-shoot ball physics. Skip entirely; if the team wants a "satisfaction · animation" angle, brief a clean knockdown-chain montage with the toy gun and dummies instead.

The bus-sorting puzzle hook is a fundamentally different mechanic (sort/path-clearing puzzle) from Knock'em All's tap-to-shoot physics ragdoll loop, so reusing this concept would mislead installs and hurt D1 retention. Palette and casual-relaxation framing share some hypercasual audience overlap, but the top-down stylus interaction, muted puzzle palette, and 'relaxing' pitch clash with the game's first-person aiming, vibrant red dummies, and punchy comic-book hit feedback. Recommend skipping a direct copy; if testing the 'satisfaction' angle, rebuild around oddly-satisfying ragdoll knockdowns with the VO question hook only.

This documentary-style "secret bunker beneath the Eiffel Tower" cross-section hook has almost no connection to Knock'em All's tap-to-shoot physics loop — there's no shooting, no dummies, no playable demonstration the user could mimic. The dark earthy palette (#2c3e50, #d35400, #2d5a27) also clashes hard with the game's bright pastel/primary look, and the cluster is tiny (1 creative, 1 advertiser, 328d old) so there's no proven scale signal. Skip unless we can heavily reskin it into a "what's hidden behind/under the platform" reveal that ties directly to knocking dummies off — otherwise the curiosity hook won't convert installs for this title.

The "fail at a bus puzzle" hook is a draw-puzzle/parking trope that has zero mechanical overlap with Knock'em All's tap-to-shoot physics loop — players clicking the ad expecting vehicle steering will bounce hard, hurting CVR and D1. Visual palette (white/green/red) is partially compatible with the game's bright flat aesthetic, but the bus/cursor staging misrepresents the FPS dummy-shooting gameplay. Only the broad casual "fail bait" audience overlaps; recommend skipping this archetype and instead testing fail-bait framed around missed shots or dummies surviving on the platform.

brief.title: "Ever Played Something This Satisfying?" — Ragdoll ASMR Hook
brief.hook_3s: VO whispers "Ever played a game that actually feels this satisfying?" as a single tap launches a ball that pancakes a row of red dummies in slow-mo.
scene_flow: 0-3s HOOK: First-person view down the toy gun barrel on a pastel blue platform; a finger taps and a ball arcs in slow-mo toward three red crash-test dummies. VO question lands as the first dummy ragdolls off the edge.
3-7s ESCALATION: Speed normalizes — rapid tap-tap-tap chain knockouts, comic 'BIM' 'POW' bursts pop on each hit, progress bar fills, dummies cartwheel off geometric platforms.
7-11s PAYOFF: Slow-mo replay of a chain reaction where one dummy bowls into four others — exaggerated physics, soft thuds, satisfied exhale on the VO.
11-14s FAKE-CHOICE BEAT: Final boss-dummy on a tiny platform; cursor hovers between two ball trajectories — player picks the wall-bounce trick shot, dummy flies into the void.
14-15s CTA: Progress bar snaps to 100%, screen flashes accent purple, logo + 'Tap to Play' lockup.
brief.visual_direction: Lock to the game's authentic first-person aiming POV with the colorful toy-like gun in the lower frame. Pastel sky-blue (#8ED4FF) sky and platforms dominate, vibrant red (#D91A1A) dummies pop as the only saturated targets, and accent purple (#6B15E8) drives the progress bar, comic hit-text outlines, and CTA flash. Keep environments minimalist 3D with flat-shaded geometric platforms — no textures, no clutter. Slow-mo ragdoll moments use a subtle motion-blur trail in purple. UI chrome (top-center level pill, bottom progress bar, comic 'BIM' bursts) must match in-game exactly so the ad reads as real gameplay, not a render.
text_overlays: Ever played a game this satisfying?
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current Knock'em All rotation is all unlabeled Applovin video/playable with no VO-question hook — they're missing the trending "satisfaction + relatable VO question" angle that's winning in the market scan. This brief grafts that VO hook onto the game's actual ragdoll-knockdown loop (not a foreign sort puzzle), preserving install intent while opening a fresh creative lane. UA should A/B this against the existing silent playables to test whether VO-led satisfaction lifts CTR without tanking D1.
scenario_prompts: Hero frame, aspect 9:16, authentic in-game screenshot of Knock'em All: first-person perspective looking down a chunky colorful toy-like gun barrel, three vibrant red crash-test dummies (#D91A1A) standing on a simple flat-shaded geometric platform floating in a pastel sky-blue (#8ED4FF) void, a single ball mid-flight with a soft accent-purple (#6B15E8) motion trail, comic-book 'BIM' burst exploding on the lead dummy, level progress bar UI pinned to bottom, top-center level number pill, minimalist 3D with bright flat colors and simple geometric environments, no textures, on-screen text 'Ever played a game this satisfying?' in bold white with purple outline at top. [Audio: VO (warm female, near-whisper) 'Ever played a game that actually feels this satisfying?' / chill lo-fi tap-house 80 BPM, calm and pillowy / soft whoosh on ball release at 1s, deep thud + comic 'BIM' on dummy impact at 2.5s.]

Storyboard frame 2, aspect 9:16, authentic Knock'em All gameplay still: slow-motion chain-reaction shot, one red dummy (#D91A1A) ragdolling sideways into four more dummies bowling-pin style off a pastel blue (#8ED4FF) hexagonal platform, accent-purple (#6B15E8) progress bar filling visibly at bottom, multiple comic 'POW' and 'BIM' bursts stacked, toy gun barrel still visible bottom-center in first-person, minimalist 3D flat-color geometric environment with no textures, on-screen text 'Chain reaction 💥' centered in playful bold white. [Audio: VO 'oh that's nice…' satisfied exhale / same lo-fi 80 BPM with a low sub-bass swell / stacked rubbery thud SFX, ascending chime on combo at 2s, progress-bar ding at 4s.]

Storyboard frame 3 CTA, aspect 9:16, authentic Knock'em All end-card moment: first-person view, final red crash-test dummy (#D91A1A) flying off-screen against pastel blue (#8ED4FF) sky, progress bar snapped to 100% glowing accent purple (#6B15E8), screen edges flashing soft purple, Knock'em All logo lockup centered, minimalist 3D bright flat colors simple geometric environment, on-screen text 'Tap to Play' in large bold white with purple drop shadow at bottom. [Audio: VO 'your turn.' / lo-fi resolves with a clean major chord / triumphant chime at 0.5s, soft UI tap-tick under CTA at 3s.]

brief.title: "Why Is Level 7 So Hard?" — Missed Shot Fail-Bait
brief.hook_3s: A shaky finger taps fire — the ball arcs WIDE, sails past three red dummies, and clangs off the platform edge. "WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?"
scene_flow: 0–3s: First-person view down the toy gun barrel. Three red crash-test dummies march forward on a pastel blue platform. A hand cursor jitters, taps fire — the ball flies high and misses everything. Overlay: 'WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?'
3–7s: Second attempt — cursor over-corrects, ball rolls under the dummies' feet, bounces off the back wall. Dummies keep advancing. Progress bar barely moves. Overlay: 'CAN YOU WIN?'
7–11s: Dummies reach the player. Screen shakes, comic 'BIM' text flashes red, fail screen appears with sad trombone energy. Overlay: '99% FAIL HERE'
11–14s: Cut to a clean, satisfying retry — perfectly aimed shot knocks all three dummies off the edge in a domino chain. Purple combo flash. Overlay: 'YOUR TURN'
14–15s: CTA card with download button pulsing in accent purple.
brief.visual_direction: Lock to the game's authentic first-person perspective: pastel #8ED4FF sky-blue backdrop, simple geometric platform, vivid #D91A1A red crash-test dummies, and the signature toy-like gun in lower frame. Use #6B15E8 purple sparingly for combo flashes, progress-bar fill, and CTA accents. Keep everything minimalist 3D with flat shading — no gradients, no realism. Comic-book 'BIM' hit text and the level progress bar must appear exactly as in-game. Hand cursor overlay (the fail-bait tell) sits on top of otherwise pixel-faithful gameplay.
text_overlays: WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current Knock'em All rotation is all straight gameplay videos and playables with zero fail-bait framing — we're leaving the highest-CTR hook in hypercasual on the table. This brief keeps the winning "WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD" psychological trigger but swaps the mechanically-mismatched bus-crash for a missed-shot fail that 100% matches the tap-to-shoot loop, protecting CVR and D1. UA team: test against the 2025-09-24 video as control, expect lift on IPM via thumbnail curiosity gap.
scenario_prompts: Hero frame, 9:16 vertical aspect, first-person view down a chunky toy-like cartoon gun barrel aimed at three vivid red crash-test dummy enemies (#D91A1A) walking on a simple geometric platform, pastel sky-blue background (#8ED4FF), a white hand cursor hovering over the fire button mid-tap, a yellow ball mid-flight clearly missing the dummies and arcing past them, thin level progress bar at top with #6B15E8 purple fill barely started, minimalist 3D with bright flat colors and simple geometric environments, no characters on screen, comic-book style overlay text 'WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?' in bold white with black outline at top center, authentic Knock'em All in-game UI chrome. [Audio: VO (frustrated female, casual): 'Wait, why is this so hard?' / calm satisfying lo-fi tap-beat 90 BPM / soft whoosh on ball, hollow clank on miss at 2.5s.]

Storyboard frame 2, 9:16 vertical aspect, first-person Knock'em All gameplay, three red crash-test dummies (#D91A1A) up close to the camera mid-collision with the player, screen-shake motion blur, large comic-book 'BIM' hit text in red and white burst across center, pastel #8ED4FF background, geometric platform edges visible, level progress bar frozen with #6B15E8 purple fill at 20%, minimalist 3D flat-color style, no characters on screen, overlay text '99% FAIL HERE' in bold white at bottom, faithful in-game HUD. [Audio: VO: 'Nooo!' / calm satisfying beat drops to muted / cartoon fail-buzzer at 0.4s, dummy thud impact, soft sad-trombone tail.]

Storyboard frame 3 redemption shot, 9:16 vertical aspect, first-person view of toy gun firing a perfectly aimed yellow ball knocking three red crash-test dummies (#D91A1A) off a geometric platform in a satisfying domino chain, bodies tumbling into pastel #8ED4FF void, bright #6B15E8 purple combo-flash radiating from impact, level progress bar filling to 100% in purple, minimalist 3D bright flat colors, no characters on screen, bold overlay text 'YOUR TURN — TAP TO PLAY' in white with purple drop shadow at bottom center, authentic Knock'em All UI. [Audio: VO (confident): 'Your turn.' / calm uplifting chiptune swell 100 BPM / triple chime combo at 1s, satisfying whoosh-pop on each dummy knockoff.]

brief.title: What's Under The Platform? — Cross-Section Reveal
brief.hook_3s: "What's hidden BENEATH the platform?" — camera slices the pastel world in half, revealing a secret multi-floor dummy factory underground.
scene_flow: 0-3s: First-person Knock'em All POV — toy gun aimed at red dummies on a sky-blue platform. Tap-tap, two dummies fall off the edge. Hook text appears.

3-7s: Camera pulls back and the ground SLICES open in a clean cross-section reveal — beneath the bright platform sits a 3-story underground 'dummy factory': conveyor belts, stacks of red crash-test dummies, purple elevators feeding new enemies up to the surface.

7-11s: Cut back to FPV. Now the player understands — shoot the support beams visible in the cross-section. A single tap collapses a floor, and a whole column of dummies tumbles in chained physics ragdoll chaos. 'BIM' 'BAM' comic pops.
11-14s: Progress bar at top fills to 100%, level-clear confetti in #6B15E8 and #D91A1A. Cross-section seals back up — clean pastel world restored.
14-15s: CTA card with toy gun icon and finger-tap prompt.
brief.visual_direction: Lean fully into Knock'em All's signature minimalist 3D: airy #8ED4FF pastel sky backdrops, flat-shaded geometric platforms, vibrant #D91A1A crash-test dummies, and #6B15E8 accents on the underground machinery and elevators. The cross-section cut should feel like a clean diagram-style slice (think architectural blueprint meets toy diorama) — no dirt, no realism, just stacked colored geometry. Preserve the exact in-game HUD: top progress bar, comic-book "BIM" hit pops, first-person toy gun in lower frame. Camera language is smooth, satisfying, low-friction — no shake, no grit.
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current Knock'em All rotation is 100% straight gameplay playables/videos with no narrative curiosity hook — this brings the trending "hidden world reveal" angle they don't yet have, while staying mechanically honest by tying the reveal directly to the tap-to-shoot loop (shoot supports → chain physics collapse). UA team: run this against broad casual lookalikes as a top-funnel curiosity opener, then retarget non-installers with the existing pure-gameplay playables to close.
scenario_prompts: Hero frame, vertical 9:16 mobile ad still, in-game moment from Knock'em All hypercasual shooter. First-person POV: a colorful toy-like gun in the lower foreground aimed across a bright #8ED4FF pastel sky platform with red #D91A1A crash-test dummy enemies standing in a row. The ground is sliced open in a clean cross-section diagram reveal showing a 3-story underground dummy factory beneath — conveyor belts, stacked red dummies, #6B15E8 purple elevators. Minimalist 3D with bright, flat colors and simple geometric environments. Top of screen shows a level progress bar UI. Bold white on-screen text: 'What's UNDER the platform?'. No characters on screen besides dummies. Clean, toy-diorama lighting, no grit. [Audio: VO (curious female whisper) 'Wait… what's under there?' / calm satisfying lo-fi 90bpm with soft mallet melody / soft whoosh as ground slices open at 2s, subtle mechanical hum from factory].

Storyboard frame 2, vertical 9:16, in-game Knock'em All FPV. The toy gun fires a ball at a thin purple #6B15E8 support beam visible through the cross-section cut; a whole stack of red #D91A1A crash-test dummies is mid-collapse in physics ragdoll chaos, tumbling between underground floors. Bright #8ED4FF pastel sky above the slice. Comic-book style hit text 'BIM' pops in yellow. Level progress bar UI at top filling toward 100%. Minimalist 3D with bright, flat colors and simple geometric environments. On-screen text overlay: 'Shoot the supports…'. [Audio: VO 'One shot brings it ALL down' / upbeat satisfying 100bpm soft-pop with handclaps / pop-pop tap SFX, wooden domino tumble cascade, 'BIM' comic stinger at 1.5s].

Storyboard frame 3, vertical 9:16, end-card style still from Knock'em All. The cross-section has sealed back into a clean pastel #8ED4FF platform, confetti in #D91A1A red and #6B15E8 purple bursts across the frame, level progress bar UI at top shows 100% complete. First-person toy gun rests in lower frame, last red crash-test dummy ragdolls off the platform edge. Minimalist 3D with bright, flat colors and simple geometric environments. Large bold white text overlay: 'Tap to Play' with a tapping finger icon. No human character on screen. [Audio: VO 'Your turn — tap to play' / cheerful satisfying chime resolution 95bpm / confetti pop SFX, soft success chime, single finger-tap click at 4.5s].
